fr:frontmatter.fr:title: The partial map classifier is a Segal type

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: For any i: \Delta ^{ 1 }, we will write A^i for the function space { \mathopen {} \left ( i=1 \right ) \mathclose {}} \to A.
Given a type A, let LA be the \Delta ^{ 1 }-partial map classifier specified below:
fr:mainmatter.fr:tex: LA : \equiv \mathchoice { \textstyle \sum _{ { \mathopen {} \left ( i: \Delta ^{ 1 } \right ) \mathclose {}} } }{ \textstyle \sum _{ { \mathopen {} \left ( i: \Delta ^{ 1 } \right ) \mathclose {}} } }{ \scriptstyle \sum _{ { \mathopen {} \left ( i: \Delta ^{ 1 } \right ) \mathclose {}} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \sum _{ { \mathopen {} \left ( i: \Delta ^{ 1 } \right ) \mathclose {}} } } A^i
fr:mainmatter.fr:p: We first claim that if A is a Segal type, then LA is also a Segal type; we must prove that the precomposition map { \mathopen {} \left [ \epsilon ,LA \right ] \mathclose {}} \colon { \mathopen {} \left [ \Delta ^{ 2 } ,LA \right ] \mathclose {}} \to { \mathopen {} \left [ \Lambda ^{ 2 }_{ 1 } ,LA \right ] \mathclose {}} is an equivalence.
We first give a more explicit computation of what a triangle in LA is.
fr:mainmatter.fr:tex: \begin {aligned} { \mathopen {} \left [ \Delta ^{ 2 } ,LA \right ] \mathclose {}} & \equiv { \mathopen {} \left [ \Delta ^{ 2 } , \mathchoice { \textstyle \sum _{ { \mathopen {} \left ( i: \Delta ^{ 1 } \right ) \mathclose {}} } }{ \textstyle \sum _{ { \mathopen {} \left ( i: \Delta ^{ 1 } \right ) \mathclose {}} } }{ \scriptstyle \sum _{ { \mathopen {} \left ( i: \Delta ^{ 1 } \right ) \mathclose {}} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \sum _{ { \mathopen {} \left ( i: \Delta ^{ 1 } \right ) \mathclose {}} } } A^i \right ] \mathclose {}} \\ & \cong \mathchoice { \textstyle \sum _{ { \mathopen {} \left ( u: { \mathopen {} \left [ \Delta ^{ 2 } , \Delta ^{ 1 } \right ] \mathclose {}} \right ) \mathclose {}} } }{ \textstyle \sum _{ { \mathopen {} \left ( u: { \mathopen {} \left [ \Delta ^{ 2 } , \Delta ^{ 1 } \right ] \mathclose {}} \right ) \mathclose {}} } }{ \scriptstyle \sum _{ { \mathopen {} \left ( u: { \mathopen {} \left [ \Delta ^{ 2 } , \Delta ^{ 1 } \right ] \mathclose {}} \right ) \mathclose {}} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \sum _{ { \mathopen {} \left ( u: { \mathopen {} \left [ \Delta ^{ 2 } , \Delta ^{ 1 } \right ] \mathclose {}} \right ) \mathclose {}} } } \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ { \mathopen {} \left ( v: \Delta ^{ 2 } \right ) \mathclose {}} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ { \mathopen {} \left ( v: \Delta ^{ 2 } \right ) \mathclose {}} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ { \mathopen {} \left ( v: \Delta ^{ 2 } \right ) \mathclose {}} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ { \mathopen {} \left ( v: \Delta ^{ 2 } \right ) \mathclose {}} } } A^{uv} \end {aligned}
fr:mainmatter.fr:p: By means of a version of the Phoa principle that I have not written down carefully yet, we should have an equivalence \Phi _2 \colon { \mathopen {} \left [ \Delta ^{ 2 } , \Delta ^{ 1 } \right ] \mathclose {}} \to \Delta ^{ 3 } sending h \colon \Delta ^{ 2 } \to \Delta ^{ 1 } to the following chain: \Phi _2 h: \equiv { \mathopen {} \left ( h { \mathopen {} \left ( 0,0 \right ) \mathclose {}} \leq h { \mathopen {} \left ( 0,1 \right ) \mathclose {}} \leq h { \mathopen {} \left ( 1,1 \right ) \mathclose {}} \right ) \mathclose {}} Conversely, a chain { \mathopen {} \left ( i \leq j \leq k \right ) \mathclose {}} \colon \Delta ^{ 3 } is identified with the map h \colon \Delta ^{ 2 } \to \Delta ^{ 1 } sending { \mathopen {} \left ( l \leq m \right ) \mathclose {}} : \Delta ^{ 2 } to the connection i \lor { \mathopen {} \left ( j \land l \right ) \mathclose {}} \lor { \mathopen {} \left ( k \land m \right ) \mathclose {}} . That these operations are mutually inverse seems to be the essence of the Phoa principle.
Therefore we have our final computation of triangles in LA:
fr:mainmatter.fr:tex: \begin {aligned} { \mathopen {} \left [ \Delta ^{ 2 } ,LA \right ] \mathclose {}} & \cong \mathchoice { \textstyle \sum _{ { \mathopen {} \left ( i \leq j \leq k \right ) \mathclose {}} } }{ \textstyle \sum _{ { \mathopen {} \left ( i \leq j \leq k \right ) \mathclose {}} } }{ \scriptstyle \sum _{ { \mathopen {} \left ( i \leq j \leq k \right ) \mathclose {}} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \sum _{ { \mathopen {} \left ( i \leq j \leq k \right ) \mathclose {}} } } \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ { \mathopen {} \left ( l \leq m \right ) \mathclose {}} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ { \mathopen {} \left ( l \leq m \right ) \mathclose {}} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ { \mathopen {} \left ( l \leq m \right ) \mathclose {}} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ { \mathopen {} \left ( l \leq m \right ) \mathclose {}} } } { \mathopen {} \left ( \Phi _2 ^{-1} { \mathopen {} \left ( i,j,k \right ) \mathclose {}} \, { \mathopen {} \left ( l,m \right ) \mathclose {}} =1 \right ) \mathclose {}} \to A \\ & \cong \mathchoice { \textstyle \sum _{ { \mathopen {} \left ( i \leq j \leq k \right ) \mathclose {}} } }{ \textstyle \sum _{ { \mathopen {} \left ( i \leq j \leq k \right ) \mathclose {}} } }{ \scriptstyle \sum _{ { \mathopen {} \left ( i \leq j \leq k \right ) \mathclose {}} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \sum _{ { \mathopen {} \left ( i \leq j \leq k \right ) \mathclose {}} } } \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ { \mathopen {} \left ( l \leq m \right ) \mathclose {}} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ { \mathopen {} \left ( l \leq m \right ) \mathclose {}} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ { \mathopen {} \left ( l \leq m \right ) \mathclose {}} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ { \mathopen {} \left ( l \leq m \right ) \mathclose {}} } } A^{i \lor { \mathopen {} \left ( j \land l \right ) \mathclose {}} \lor { \mathopen {} \left ( k \land m \right ) \mathclose {}} } \end {aligned}
fr:mainmatter.fr:p: By much the same token, we can compute what a horn is in LA. Using similar reasoning to the above (as well as the fact \Delta ^{ 1 } is Segal), we have:
fr:mainmatter.fr:tex: \begin {aligned} { \mathopen {} \left [ \Lambda ^{ 2 }_{ 1 } ,LA \right ] \mathclose {}} & \cong \mathchoice { \textstyle \sum _{ { \mathopen {} \left ( i \leq j \leq k \right ) \mathclose {}} } }{ \textstyle \sum _{ { \mathopen {} \left ( i \leq j \leq k \right ) \mathclose {}} } }{ \scriptstyle \sum _{ { \mathopen {} \left ( i \leq j \leq k \right ) \mathclose {}} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \sum _{ { \mathopen {} \left ( i \leq j \leq k \right ) \mathclose {}} } } \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ { \mathopen {} \left ( l \leq m \mid l=0 \lor m=1 \right ) \mathclose {}} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ { \mathopen {} \left ( l \leq m \mid l=0 \lor m=1 \right ) \mathclose {}} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ { \mathopen {} \left ( l \leq m \mid l=0 \lor m=1 \right ) \mathclose {}} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ { \mathopen {} \left ( l \leq m \mid l=0 \lor m=1 \right ) \mathclose {}} } } A^{i \lor { \mathopen {} \left ( j \land l \right ) \mathclose {}} \lor { \mathopen {} \left ( k \land m \right ) \mathclose {}} } \end {aligned}
fr:mainmatter.fr:p: Under the identifications above, the horn restriction map { \mathopen {} \left [ \epsilon ,LA \right ] \mathclose {}} \colon { \mathopen {} \left [ \Delta ^{ 2 } ,LA \right ] \mathclose {}} \to { \mathopen {} \left [ \Lambda ^{ 2 }_{ 1 } ,LA \right ] \mathclose {}} simply forgets the additional assumption l=0 \lor m=1. To construct an inverse to this map, we will ultimately use the inverse to { \mathopen {} \left [ \epsilon ,A \right ] \mathclose {}} \colon { \mathopen {} \left [ \Delta ^{ 2 } ,A \right ] \mathclose {}} \to { \mathopen {} \left [ \Lambda ^{ 2 }_{ 1 } ,A \right ] \mathclose {}} that we obtain from our assumption that A is a Segal type. In particular, we define { \mathopen {} \left [ \epsilon ,LA \right ] \mathclose {}} ^{-1} \colon { \mathopen {} \left [ \Lambda ^{ 2 }_{ 1 } ,A \right ] \mathclose {}} \to { \mathopen {} \left [ \Delta ^{ 2 } ,LA \right ] \mathclose {}} to take i \leq j \leq k and a: \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ { \mathopen {} \left ( l \leq m \mid l=0 \lor m=1 \right ) \mathclose {}} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ { \mathopen {} \left ( l \leq m \mid l=0 \lor m=1 \right ) \mathclose {}} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ { \mathopen {} \left ( l \leq m \mid l=0 \lor m=1 \right ) \mathclose {}} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ { \mathopen {} \left ( l \leq m \mid l=0 \lor m=1 \right ) \mathclose {}} } } A^{i \lor { \mathopen {} \left ( j \land l \right ) \mathclose {}} \lor { \mathopen {} \left ( k \land m \right ) \mathclose {}} } to the following triangle in LA:
fr:mainmatter.fr:tex: { \mathopen {} \left ( i \leq j \leq k, \lambda l \leq m \mathpunct {.} \begin {cases} i=1 \hookrightarrow { \mathopen {} \left ( { \mathopen {} \left [ \epsilon ,A \right ] \mathclose {}} ^{-1} a \right ) \mathclose {}} { \mathopen {} \left ( l,m \right ) \mathclose {}} \\ { \mathopen {} \left ( j=l=1 \right ) \mathclose {}} \lor { \mathopen {} \left ( k=m=1 \right ) \mathclose {}} \hookrightarrow a { \mathopen {} \left ( l,m \right ) \mathclose {}} \end {cases} \right ) \mathclose {}}
